nativeRange.habitat: In the wild, the Galaxy Pleco occupies fast-flowing, well-oxygenated river rapids over rocky substrates — a lifestyle described as reophilic (rapids-loving). It is typically found among submerged wood and rock crevices, which it uses as daytime refuges.

decorPreferences: smooth sand or fine gravel substrate to protect the soft underbelly
multiple caves, PVC tubes, or stacked slate offering tight retreats
driftwood and root tangles for cover and biofilm grazing surface
smooth river rock and boulders arranged to create territory boundaries
strong directional flow from powerheads, spray bars, or wavemakers to replicate rapids
highly efficient filtration with excess oxygenation
subdued lighting or shaded areas to encourage daytime activity

diet.notes: Unlike the algae-grazing plecos commonly sold in the trade (such as Pterygoplichthys and Ancistrus), the Galaxy Pleco is a true carnivore, adapted to crushing and eating snails and other small invertebrates in the wild. The two prominent upper-jaw tusks — the source of the 'Vampire' trade name — are used to grip and extract soft prey. Captive diet should center on protein-rich foods: sinking carnivore pellets, frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp, mysis, chopped prawn and mussel, earthworms, and live or frozen snails. The Galaxy Pleco should not be relied on for algae control. Feeding primarily after lights out matches its nocturnal activity pattern.

goodWith: larger characins and tetras occupying the upper water column
reophilic cichlids (e.g. Retroculus, Geophagus, larger Apistogramma)
active mid-water dither fish that share the rapids biotope
other large Loricariids only if each has its own dedicated cave territory
avoidWith: other bottom-dwelling catfish that compete for caves (Corydoras, other plecos in small tanks)
shrimp and other small soft-bodied invertebrates
conspecifics in tanks under ~350 L without multiple non-overlapping territories
slow-moving or fragile fish that may be outcompeted at feeding
species requiring alkaline hard water (incompatible parameters)

summary: The Galaxy Pleco (L029), also widely sold as the Vampire or Tusken Pleco, is a dark-bodied Loricariid covered in dense white-to-yellow spots, with two prominent fang-like upper-jaw teeth that give it its name. Native to Amazonian and Orinoco rapids, it is a nocturnal true carnivore generally considered intermediate in care.
careNotes:
Leporacanthicus galaxias is one of the most iconic L-number plecos in the hobby, distinguished by its dark body covered in dense pale spotting and the pair of enlarged upper-jaw teeth (odontotes) used to extract snails and other invertebrate prey — the origin of the 'Vampire' and 'Tusken' trade names. As the first described species in the Leporacanthicus genus, it is a useful reference point for understanding what sets carnivorous plecos apart from the algae-grazing types many keepers expect.

The species is reophilic (rapids-loving): wild populations occupy fast-flowing, well-oxygenated river sections in the southern tributaries of the middle and lower Amazon, the Madeira, Tocantins and Guamá drainages, and the Ventuari River in the upper Orinoco basin (Venezuela). In captivity it requires the same conditions — strong directional flow from powerheads or a spray bar, highly efficient filtration with excess oxygenation, and a smooth sand or fine-gravel substrate that will not abrade the soft underbelly. Caves (PVC tubes 5–8 cm in diameter work well during acclimatisation, with slate stacks or ceramic cave tubes for permanent territories) and driftwood are essential, both as daytime refuges and as territorial markers.

Galaxy Plecos are generally peaceful toward fish occupying other strata of the tank but are markedly territorial toward other benthic species — particularly other plecos, large catfish, and conspecifics. A single adult is suitable for a tank from roughly 120 × 45 × 45 cm (~250 L). Keeping multiple specimens or mixing with other large Loricariids requires significantly larger footprints (350 L+) with several non-overlapping cave territories. The species is mostly nocturnal; aquarists who see little daytime activity should not be alarmed.

Water chemistry should be soft to moderately hard (gH ~2–12) and slightly acidic to neutral (pH ~5.8–7.2), at tropical temperatures of around 22–26°C. As a wild-caught carnivore, this species is sensitive to dissolved-organic accumulation and benefits from large frequent water changes. Like most scaled Loricariids it is moderately copper-sensitive; copper-based medications should be used with caution. Adult standard length is approximately 22–25 cm (FishBase records up to 21.1 cm SL; aquarium sources commonly cite ~25 cm). Note that some retailers market the related L240 ('Galaxy Vampire Pleco', generally referable to a distinct Leporacanthicus species) under the same common names — verify the L-number when purchasing.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has been reported but remains uncommon and details are scarce in the literature. Like other Ancistrinae, Leporacanthicus galaxias is a cave spawner — males select and guard a cave, the female deposits adhesive eggs inside, and the male fans and defends the clutch through hatching. Conditioning a sexed group on protein-rich live and frozen foods, providing multiple suitable cave shapes, and replicating rainy-season triggers (large cool, soft water changes) are the techniques generally advised. Sexing is possible in mature fish: males develop a noticeably longer and wider head, a larger dorsal fin, and more pronounced odontotes (bristles) along the pectoral spines and head. Breeding is generally considered difficult and is undertaken mainly by specialist keepers.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Leporacanthicus galaxias
primary.notes: Used as primary because PlanetCatfish (the slice's brief-designated primary for L-number plecos) returned HTTP 403 on every attempted fetch. SeriouslyFish provided native range, water parameters (22-26°C, pH 5.6-7.0, gH 0-10), 25 cm SL maximum size, minimum tank size (120x45x45 cm / ~243 L), reophilic habitat, peaceful-but-territorial-to-benthos temperament, cave/driftwood/sand decor requirements, and sexual dimorphism notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Leporacanthicus galaxias
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Loricariidae, Siluriformes), authoritative scientific authority (Isbrücker & Nijssen 1989), maximum size (21.1 cm SL), tropical temperature range (22-25°C), pH range (6.2-7.2), and confirmed native range across the southern Amazon tributaries, Madeira, Tocantins, Guamá, and Ventuari/Orinoco drainages.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory — Vampire Pleco / Galaxy Pleco Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (9-10 inches / ~23-25 cm), lifespan estimate (up to ~15 years), temperature (72-80°F), pH (6.5-7.0), nocturnal behavior, carnivorous diet emphasis, and 'avoid other bottom-dwellers and snails' tankmate guidance.

additional.name: The Aquarium Wiki — Vampire Pleco
additional.notes: Secondary corroboration of trade-level care parameters and L-number nomenclature (L029, L007). Cross-checked tank size minimum (~250 L) and reinforced carnivore diet recommendation.

fish.breedingNotes: The Galaxy Pleco is a cave spawner in which the male selects and guards the site, then fans and defends the eggs and newly hatched fry. Conditioning the pair on protein-rich foods, providing multiple cave shapes to allow female choice, and simulating rainy-season triggers with large cool soft-water changes are the approaches most commonly advised. Successful captive spawning has been reported but remains uncommon outside specialist keepers.
